title: Rickettsial Diseases
section: Diseases Caused by Rickettsiae Mycoplasmas and Chlamydiae
authors: David H. Walker, J. Stephen Dumler, Lucas S. Blanton, Chantal P. Rovers

items.title: Recognizing Rickettsial Infections: Key Clinical Features
items.content: Rickettsial infections present with non-specific early symptoms such as fever, headache, and myalgias. As the disease progresses, manifestations can include rash (macular, maculopapular, vesicular), eschar at tick bite site, pneumonitis, meningoencephalitis, or multiorgan failure.
keyPoints: Acute symptoms: fever, headache, myalgias, nausea, vomiting, cough
Progressive manifestations: rash (macular, maculopapular, vesicular), eschar, pneumonitis, meningoencephalitis, hypotension, multiorgan failure
Diseases with specific features: Q fever and severe RMSF can cause pneumonitis; louse-borne typhus and severe cases of other rickettsioses may lead to meningoencephalitis
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · Rickettsial Diseases

items.title: Empirical Treatment for Rickettsial Infections: Doxycycline as First-Line Therapy
items.content: Given the difficulty in establishing a definitive diagnosis during the acute stage of rickettsioses, empirical treatment with doxycycline is recommended based on clinical suspicion supported by epidemiologic data and characteristic laboratory findings.
keyPoints: Empirical drug of choice: Doxycycline
Clinical suspicion indicators: fever, rash or eschar, thrombocytopenia, normal/low WBC counts, elevated hepatic enzymes, hyponatremia
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · Rickettsial Diseases

items.title: Epidemiologic Clues for Rickettsial Pathogen Transmission
items.content: Identifying the transmission vector and geographic exposure is crucial in diagnosing rickettsial diseases. Key epidemiologic clues include environmental exposure to vectors, travel to endemic regions, and specific animal exposures.
keyPoints: Environmental exposure: ticks, fleas, mites during active season
Geographic factors: travel or residence in endemic areas
Animal exposures: parturient ruminants, cats, dogs (Q fever), flying squirrels (R. prowazekii)
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · Rickettsial Diseases

items.subtopic: TICK-， MITE-， LOUSE-， AND FLEA-BORNE RICKETTSIOES
items.title: Understanding the Case-Fatality Rates of Rickettsial Diseases
items.content: Rickettsial diseases, caused by organisms in the genera Rickettsia and Orientia, vary significantly in their case-fatality rates. The most life-threatening infections are ordered from highest to lowest case-fatality rate as follows: R. rickettsii (Rocky Mountain spotted fever), R. prowazekii (louse-borne typhus), Orientia tsutsugamushi (scrub typhus), R. conorii (Mediterranean spotted fever), and R. typhi (murine typhus).
keyPoints: Rickettsial diseases result from endothelial cell infection.
Case-fatality rates are highest for R. rickettsii, followed by R. prowazekii, Orientia tsutsugamushi, R. conorii, and R. typhi.
Some agents like R. parkeri and R. africae have never been documented to cause fatal illness.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · TICK-， MITE-， LOUSE-， AND FLEA-BORNE RICKETTSIOES

items.subtopic: TICK-， MITE-， LOUSE-， AND FLEA-BORNE RICKETTSIOES
items.title: Differentiating Between Similar Rickettsial Species
items.content: Many rickettsial species, such as R. africae, R. parkeri, and R. sibirica, are so similar that they do not require separate descriptions. The similarities among MSF (R. conorii [all strains] and R. massiliae), North Asian tick typhus (R. sibirica), Japanese spotted fever (R. japonica), and Flinders Island spotted fever (R. honei) are more significant than their minor variations.
keyPoints: Reductive evolution has led to small genomes in pathogenic rickettsial species.
Many rickettsial diseases are grouped based on lipopolysaccharide antigens into typhus and spotted fever groups.
Similarities among certain rickettsial species outweigh their minor variations.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · TICK-， MITE-， LOUSE-， AND FLEA-BORNE RICKETTSIOES

items.title: Epidemiology and Mortality Rates
items.content: RMSF is prevalent in specific regions of the United States, Canada, Mexico, and Central/South America. The mortality rate has decreased from 20–25% preantibiotics to ~3–5% postantibiotics due to delayed diagnosis and treatment. However, true case-fatality rates are higher (9-10%) where R. rickettsii is the sole infecting species.
keyPoints: RMSF occurs in 47 states with highest prevalence in south-central and southeastern U.S., Canada, Mexico, Central/South America
Mortality rate was 20–25% preantibiotics; ~3–5% postantibiotics due to delayed diagnosis/treatment
Reported case-fatality rates: 9% (U.S.), 10% (parts of Arizona), higher in children (<10 years) and elderly (>70 years)
Case-fatality rate approaches 50% in Mexico and Brazil
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 1)

items.title: Pathogenesis of RMSF
items.content: R. rickettsii is transmitted through tick bites, leading to lymphohematogenous spread and endothelial cell infection. Increased vascular permeability causes edema, hypovolemia, and ischemia, rather than occlusive thrombosis.
keyPoints: Inoculation occurs after ≥6 h of feeding
Spread is lymphohematogenous; infects endothelial cells
Increased vascular permeability leads to tissue injury
Thrombocytopenia in 32–52% of patients; DIC with hypofibrinogenemia is rare
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Rash Progression
items.content: Early symptoms mimic viral illnesses, complicating early diagnosis. Rash appears in a minority of patients initially but progresses to petechiae. Involvement of palms/soles is late or absent, impacting diagnostic timing.
keyPoints: Symptoms: fever, headache, malaise, myalgia, nausea/vomiting
Rash evident in 14% on day 1; 49% within first 3 days
Petechiae occur in 41–59%, appearing after day 6 in 74% with rash
Palms/soles involvement late (after day 5) or absent in 18–64%
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 1)

items.title: CNS Involvement in RMSF
items.content: Central nervous system involvement significantly impacts the outcome of Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever (RMSF). Encephalitis, presenting as confusion or lethargy, is observed in a substantial percentage of cases. Severe manifestations include stupor, delirium, ataxia, coma, and seizures.
keyPoints: Encephalitis occurs in 26–28% of RMSF cases.
Severe encephalitis manifests as stupor or delirium in 21–26%, ataxia in 18%, coma in 10%, and seizures in 8%.
Meningoencephalitis results in CSF pleocytosis in 34–38% of cases, with cell counts usually between 10–100 cells/μL and a mononuclear predominance.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Challenges in RMSF
items.content: Diagnosing RMSF during the acute stage is challenging due to non-specific early symptoms and low recall of tick exposure. The classic triad of fever, rash, and history of tick exposure appears only in a small percentage of patients initially.
keyPoints: Only 60% of patients recall being bitten by a tick.
The classic triad (fever, rash, tick exposure) is present in just 3% during the first 3 days.
Serologic tests are usually negative at presentation; treatment should not be delayed for serology.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 2)

items.title: Serologic Testing and Timing in RMSF
items.content: Serologic testing, specifically the indirect immunofluorescence assay, is crucial for confirming RMSF but has limitations. Reactive titers are typically not detectable until 7–10 days after onset.
keyPoints: Indirect immunofluorescence IgG assay sensitivity and specificity are 89–100% and 99–100%, respectively.
Reactive titer of ≥64 is first detectable 7–10 days post-onset.
IgM detection in early illness is not more sensitive and may show nonspecific cross-reactivity.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 2)

items.subtopic: ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 3) | Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever
items.title: Diagnostic Approach for RMSF During Acute Illness
items.content: Immunohistologic examination of a cutaneous biopsy from a rash lesion is the most useful diagnostic test during acute RMSF. A 3-mm punch biopsy has high specificity and moderate sensitivity, while PCR on a rash biopsy may offer higher sensitivity.
keyPoints: A 3-mm punch biopsy for R. rickettsii is 70% sensitive and 100% specific.
PCR on peripheral blood lacks adequate sensitivity due to low circulating quantities of rickettsiae.
Cultivation in cell culture is technically challenging and poses biohazard concerns.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 3) | Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever

items.subtopic: ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 3) | Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever
items.title: Management of RMSF with Doxycycline
items.content: Doxycycline is the drug of choice for treating RMSF in both children and adults. It should be administered immediately upon suspicion, given its effectiveness and safety profile, including minimal risk of dental staining in children.
keyPoints: Doxycycline dosage: 100 mg twice daily orally or intravenously if necessary.
For suspected RMSF in children, up to five courses can be safely administered.
Continue doxycycline until the patient is afebrile and improving clinically—usually 3–5 days after defervescence.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 3) | Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever

items.subtopic: MEDITERRANEAN SPOTTED FEVER (BOUTONNEUSE FEVER), AFRICAN TICK-BITE FEVER, AND OTHER TICK-BORNE SPOTTED FEVERS
items.title: Recognizing High-Risk Patients for Severe Mediterranean Spotted Fever
items.content: Mediterranean spotted fever can lead to severe disease with a high mortality rate in certain patient populations.
keyPoints: R. conorii causes Mediterranean spotted fever prevalent in southern Europe, Africa, and parts of Asia.
Characterized by high fever, rash, and inoculation eschar (tâche noire).
Severe form has a mortality rate of 50% in patients with diabetes, alcoholism, or heart failure.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · MEDITERRANEAN SPOTTED FEVER (BOUTONNEUSE FEVER), AFRICAN TICK-BITE FEVER, AND OTHER TICK-BORNE SPOTTED FEVERS

items.subtopic: MEDITERRANEAN SPOTTED FEVER (BOUTONNEUSE FEVER), AFRICAN TICK-BITE FEVER, AND OTHER TICK-BORNE SPOTTED FEVERS
items.title: Diagnosis of Tick-Borne Spotted Fevers
items.content: Diagnosis relies on clinical presentation and epidemiologic context, confirmed by specific laboratory tests.
keyPoints: Clinical diagnosis based on fever, rash, and skin lesions (black necrotic lesion or crust with erythema).
Confirmed by serology, immunohistochemical demonstration in biopsies, cell-culture isolation, or PCR.
Serologic tests detect antibodies to shared antigens among SFG rickettsiae, complicating species identification.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · MEDITERRANEAN SPOTTED FEVER (BOUTONNEUSE FEVER), AFRICAN TICK-BITE FEVER, AND OTHER TICK-BORNE SPOTTED FEVERS

items.title: Treatment Approach for Severe Tick-Borne Spotted Fevers
items.content: Severe cases of tick-borne spotted fevers, such as Rocky Mountain spotted fever (RMSF), should be treated with doxycycline. The treatment duration is 3–5 days after defervescence to ensure complete resolution.
keyPoints: Doxycycline dosage: 100 mg bid orally
Treatment duration for severe cases: continued for 3–5 days after defervescence
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · Tick-Borne Spotted Fevers | RICKETTSIALPOX

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Rickettsialpox
items.content: Rickettsialpox presents with a papule that develops into a 1- to 2.5-cm painless black crusted eschar, often accompanied by regional lymphadenopathy. After an incubation period of 10–17 days, symptoms include malaise, fever, and rash. Diagnosis is based on clinical, epidemiologic, and convalescent serologic data.
keyPoints: Eschar size: 1- to 2.5-cm
Incubation period: 10–17 days
Fever duration without treatment: 6–10 days
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · Tick-Borne Spotted Fevers | RICKETTSIALPOX

items.title: Recognize and Manage Epidemic Typhus in Louse-Infested Populations
items.content: Epidemic typhus, caused by R. prowazekii, is a severe illness often associated with poor hygiene conditions where body lice thrive. It presents abruptly after an incubation period of 1–2 weeks with symptoms including high fever, headache, and rash, which can be undetected in darker skin tones.
keyPoints: Epidemic typhus is linked to poor hygienic conditions and outbreaks occur in war-torn or disaster-stricken areas.
Symptoms include abrupt onset of prostration, severe headache, fever up to 40.0°C (104°F), cough in 70% of patients, and a rash that becomes maculopapular, petechial, and confluent without treatment.
Neurologic involvement occurs in 12% of cases; untreated disease has a fatality rate of 7–40%, with renal insufficiency and multiorgan involvement common.
Doxycycline (100 mg bid) is the treatment until 3–5 days after symptoms resolve, with alternative dosing strategies under epidemic conditions.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · FLEA-BORNE SPOTTED FEVER | EPIDEMIC (LOUSE-BORNE) TYPHUS

items.title: Diagnose and Differentiate Epidemic Typhus from Other Febrile Illnesses
items.content: Epidemic typhus can be misdiagnosed as other febrile illnesses like typhoid fever, especially in tropical regions. Diagnosis is confirmed by serologic or immunohistochemical methods or detection of R. prowazekii in lice.
keyPoints: Diagnosis involves recognizing the clinical presentation and confirming with serologic studies or detecting R. prowazekii in a patient's louse.
Differential diagnosis includes typhoid fever, particularly in tropical settings where serologic tests may be unavailable.
Rapid identification is crucial due to high case-fatality rates and potential for misdiagnosis.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · FLEA-BORNE SPOTTED FEVER | EPIDEMIC (LOUSE-BORNE) TYPHUS

items.title: Prevent Epidemic Typhus through Louse Control Measures
items.content: Prevention of epidemic typhus focuses on controlling body lice populations by maintaining hygiene, laundering clothes in hot water, and using insecticides.
keyPoints: Prevention strategies include regular changing and washing of clothes in hot water and applying insecticides every 6 weeks to control louse infestations.
Effective prevention is critical in areas with poor hygienic conditions where body lice are prevalent.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · FLEA-BORNE SPOTTED FEVER | EPIDEMIC (LOUSE-BORNE) TYPHUS

items.title: Epidemiology of Endemic Murine Typhus and Suspected Transmission Cycles
items.content: Endemic murine typhus is maintained in mammalian host-flea cycles, primarily involving rats and the Oriental rat flea. In the U.S., transmission may occur via an opossum-cat flea cycle, especially in Texas and southern California.
keyPoints: Rickettsia typhi is transmitted through flea feces contaminating bite lesions or inhalation of aerosolized rickettsiae.
In the U.S., murine typhus occurs mainly in Texas and southern California, with an opossum-cat flea cycle suspected.
Incidence peaks from April to July in Texas and during warm months elsewhere.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ENDEMIC MURINE TYPHUS

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Diagnostic Challenges of Endemic Murine Typhus
items.content: Endemic murine typhus presents with headache, myalgia, arthralgia, nausea, malaise, followed by chills and fever. Rash appears in about half the patients but is often faint initially.
keyPoints: Incubation period averages 11 days (range, 8–16 days).
Rash occurs in ~50% of patients, present at presentation in only 13%, and becomes maculopapular over time.
Pulmonary involvement includes cough and radiographic findings in some cases; rash is less common in darkly pigmented skin.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ENDEMIC MURINE TYPHUS

items.title: Treatment Protocols for Endemic Murine Typhus
items.content: Empirical treatment with doxycycline (100 mg twice daily by mouth for 7 days) is recommended when endemic typhus is suspected. Chloramphenicol can be used where available.
keyPoints: Doxycycline is the first-line treatment; chloramphenicol is an alternative.
Ciprofloxacin and azithromycin are less effective than doxycycline in clinical practice.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ENDEMIC MURINE TYPHUS

items.title: Epidemiology and Immunity Challenges in Scrub Typhus
items.content: Scrub typhus is caused by O. tsutsugamushi, which differs from Rickettsia species genetically and lacks lipopolysaccharide. It's transmitted via trombiculid mites (chiggers) that feed on hosts during the wet season in endemic areas like eastern/southern Asia and northern Australia. Immunity to specific strains wanes within 1–3 years, with antigenic diversity leading to loss of cross-protective immunity as short as 1 month.
keyPoints: O. tsutsugamushi differs from Rickettsia species in genetics and cell-wall composition, lacking lipopolysaccharide.
Endemic regions include eastern/southern Asia, northern Australia, western Pacific and Indian Oceans; >3% of the population is infected or reinfected monthly in some areas.
Immunity to homologous strains wanes over 1–3 years; antigenic diversity results in loss of cross-protective immunity within 1 month.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · SCRUB TYPHUS

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Diagnostic Challenges in Scrub Typhus
items.content: Scrub typhus presents with fever, headache, myalgia, cough, and gastrointestinal symptoms after 6–21 days of incubation. Classic signs like eschar and maculopapular rash are rare (<50% for eschar, <40% for rash). Severe cases may show encephalitis and interstitial pneumonia due to vascular injury. Diagnosis relies on serologic assays or PCR from eschars; treatment includes doxycycline, azithromycin, or chloramphenicol.
keyPoints: Clinical manifestations include fever, headache, myalgia, cough, gastrointestinal symptoms after 6–21 days of incubation.
<50% of returning travelers develop an eschar; <40% develop a rash on day 4–6 of illness.
Severe cases may manifest with encephalitis and interstitial pneumonia due to vascular injury.
Case-fatality rate for untreated classic cases is 6%; diagnosis via serologic assays or PCR from eschars.
Treatment options include doxycycline (100 mg twice daily for 7–15 days), azithromycin (500 mg for 3 days), or chloramphenicol (500 mg four times daily for 7–15 days).
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · SCRUB TYPHUS

items.subtopic: EHRLICHIOSES AND ANAPLASMOSES | HUMAN MONOCYTOTROPIC EHRLICHIOSIS
items.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors of Human Monocytotropic Ehrlichiosis (HME)
items.content: Human monocytotropic ehrlichiosis, caused by E. chaffeensis, is prevalent in the south-central to mid-Atlantic U.S., with cases also reported elsewhere. The disease primarily affects older adults but can be severe and fatal in children. Tick bites are common in rural areas, particularly from May through July.
keyPoints: Over 25,480 HME cases reported as of November 2023.
Incidence up to 414 per 100,000 population in some regions.
Most infections occur in south-central, southeastern, and mid-Atlantic states.
64% of infections occur from May through July.
Highest incidence in patients aged 60–84 years; 9% in children ≤19 years.
59% of HME patients are male.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · EHRLICHIOSES AND ANAPLASMOSES | HUMAN MONOCYTOTROPIC EHRLICHIOSIS

items.subtopic: EHRLICHIOSES AND ANAPLASMOSES | HUMAN MONOCYTOTROPIC EHRLICHIOSIS
items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Complications of HME
items.content: HME presents with fever, headache, myalgia, and malaise. Severe cases can lead to hospitalization in 77% of patients, with life-threatening complications such as renal failure and septic shock. Immunocompromised individuals face a higher risk of death.
keyPoints: Fever (97%), headache (70%), myalgia (68%), malaise (77%) are common symptoms.
Hospitalization in 77% of confirmed cases; mortality rate is 2%.
Severe complications include renal failure, meningoencephalitis, and septic shock.
Immunocompromised patients have a 2.3 relative risk for death.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · EHRLICHIOSES AND ANAPLASMOSES | HUMAN MONOCYTOTROPIC EHRLICHIOSIS

items.subtopic: EHRLICHIOSES AND ANAPLASMOSES | HUMAN MONOCYTOTROPIC EHRLICHIOSIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Human Monocytotropic Ehrlichiosis
items.content: Prompt empirical treatment with doxycycline is crucial for HME. Diagnosis can be suggested by fever, tick exposure, thrombocytopenia/leukopenia, and elevated serum aminotransferases. PCR amplification of E. chaffeensis nucleic acids confirms active infection.
keyPoints: Empirical doxycycline treatment reduces ICU admissions (3%) and need for mechanical ventilation (40%).
Hospital stay is reduced by 8 days, illness duration by 12 days with prompt treatment.
Diagnosis suggested by fever, tick exposure, thrombocytopenia/leukopenia, elevated serum aminotransferases.
PCR amplification of E. chaffeensis nucleic acids confirms active infection.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · EHRLICHIOSES AND ANAPLASMOSES | HUMAN MONOCYTOTROPIC EHRLICHIOSIS

items.subtopic: EWINGII EHRLICHIOSIS AND EHRLICHIA MURIS EAUCLAIRENSIS INFECTIONS | “CANDIDATUS NEOEHRLICA MIKURENSIS” INFECTION | Ehrlichioses
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Ehrlichiosis Variants
items.content: Ehrlichia ewingii and E. muris eauclatensis infections are similar to HME but less severe, often occurring in immunocompromised patients. Diagnosis is challenging due to cross-reactivity in serologic tests for E. chaffeensis. Doxycycline is effective for treatment across these variants.
keyPoints: Ehrlichia ewingii and E. muris eauclatensis infections are less severe than HME but similar.
Many cases occur in immunocompromised patients.
No specific serologic tests available; cross-reactivity with E. chaffeensis tests is common.
Doxycycline (100 mg PO or IV twice daily) is effective for treatment, reducing hospitalization rates and shortening fever duration.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · EWINGII EHRLICHIOSIS AND EHRLICHIA MURIS EAUCLAIRENSIS INFECTIONS | “CANDIDATUS NEOEHRLICHIA MIKURENSIS” INFECTION | Ehrlichioses

items.subtopic: EWINGII EHRLICHIOSIS AND EHRLICHIA MURIS EAUCLAIRENSIS INFECTIONS | “CANDIDATUS NEOEHRLICA MIKURENSIS” INFECTION | Ehrlichioses
items.title: Prevention and Management of Tick-Borne Ehrlichiosis
items.content: Preventive measures against ehrlichiosis include avoiding ticks in endemic areas, using protective clothing, tick repellents, conducting post-exposure searches, and promptly removing attached ticks.
keyPoints: Prevention involves avoidance of ticks, use of protective clothing, and tick repellents.
Prompt removal of attached ticks reduces infection risk.
Doxycycline is recommended for treatment; rifampin may be used when doxycycline is contraindicated.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · EWINGII EHRLICHIOSIS AND EHRLICHIA MURIS EAUCLAIRENSIS INFECTIONS | “CANDIDATUS NEOEHRLICHIA MIKURENSIS” INFECTION | Ehrlichioses

items.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors of HGA
items.content: Human Granulocytic Anaplasmosis (HGA) is predominantly reported in the upper Midwest and northeastern United States, with a total of 64,225 cases as of November 2023. The disease peaks from May to July but can occur year-round due to tick exposure. Males are more commonly affected (59%), and individuals over 60 years old have a sevenfold higher incidence compared to those under 15.
keyPoints: 64,225 cases reported as of November 2023
Most common in the upper Midwest and northeastern United States
Incidence peaks from May through July
Affects males more frequently (59%)
7 times higher incidence in persons older than 60 years compared to those younger than 15
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · HUMAN GRANULOCYTOTROPIC ANAPLASMOSIS

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Complications of HGA
items.content: The clinical presentation of HGA includes fever (75–100%), myalgia (73%), headache (82%), malaise (97%), with some patients experiencing nausea, vomiting, diarrhea (22–40%), cough (27%), or confusion (17%). Rash occurs in 5% of cases due to co-infection. Thrombocytopenia (80%) and leukopenia (63%) are common laboratory findings. Complications such as renal failure, acute respiratory distress syndrome, and toxic shock-like syndrome occur more frequently in the elderly and immunocompromised patients.
keyPoints: Fever present in 75–100% of cases
Myalgia (73%), headache (82%), malaise (97%) are common symptoms
Thrombocytopenia (80%) and leukopenia (63%) frequently observed
Life-threatening complications more common in elderly and immunocompromised patients
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · HUMAN GRANULOCYTOTROPIC ANAPLASMOSIS

items.title: Diagnosis of HGA
items.content: HGA should be considered for patients with influenza-like symptoms during Ixodes tick activity seasons, especially if there is a known tick bite or exposure. Diagnosis can be supported by thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, and elevated liver enzymes. PCR testing of blood before doxycycline therapy is sensitive and specific. Serodiagnosis requires a fourfold increase in antibody titer (≥128) between paired serum samples collected one month apart.
keyPoints: Include HGA in differential diagnosis during Ixodes tick activity seasons
PCR testing of blood before doxycycline therapy is sensitive and specific
Serodiagnosis requires a fourfold increase in antibody titer (≥128) between paired samples
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · HUMAN GRANULOCYTOTROPIC ANAPLASMOSIS

items.title: Treatment and Defervescence Timing in Human Granulocytotropic Anaplasmosis
items.content: Doxycycline is effective for treating HGA, with patients typically defervescing within 24-48 hours. Rifampin can be used in pregnant women and children.
keyPoints: Doxycycline (100 mg PO twice daily) is effective for HGA treatment.
Rifampin therapy is associated with improvement in pregnant women and children.
Most treated patients defervesce within 24–48 hours.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · Human Granulocytotropic Anaplasmosis | Q FEVER (part 1)

items.title: Transmission and Prevention of Human Granulocytotropic Anaplasmosis
items.content: HGA transmission can occur as few as 4 hours after a tick bite. Preventive measures include avoiding tick exposure.
keyPoints: Transmission documented as few as 4 hours post-tick bite.
Prevention requires tick avoidance.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · Human Granulocytotropic Anaplasmosis | Q FEVER (part 1)

items.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors of Q Fever
items.content: Q fever is a zoonosis primarily transmitted through inhalation from infected livestock. High-risk groups include abattoir workers, veterinarians, and farmers.
keyPoints: C. burnetii transmission typically occurs by inhalation after shedding by animals.
Primary sources are sheep, goats, and cattle, especially during parturition.
High seroprevalence in individuals with livestock contact: 65% (Canada), 72% (Netherlands) on dairy farms; 22% of US veterinarians.
Q fever is underreported globally.
Outbreaks can affect people without livestock contact.
Environmental factors and animal density influence epidemiology.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · Human Granulocytotropic Anaplasmosis | Q FEVER (part 1)

items.title: Acute Q Fever Clinical Manifestations and Complications
items.content: Acute Q fever presents with nonspecific symptoms such as fever, fatigue, photophobia, and severe headache. The presentation can vary geographically; for instance, pneumonia is more common in the Netherlands, Canada, and Croatia, while hepatitis is prevalent in endemic areas like France and Israel. Complications include pericarditis, myocarditis, and others, though they are less common. Acute Q fever during pregnancy can lead to severe outcomes such as premature birth (35%) and abortion or neonatal death (43%).
keyPoints: Incubation period: 3–30 days.
Common symptoms: fever, fatigue, photophobia, headache, chills, sweats, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea.
Cough in ~50% of Q fever pneumonia cases.
Nonspecific rash in 4–18% of patients.
WBC count usually normal; thrombocytopenia in ~25%.
Case-fatality rate: ~1–2%.
Pregnancy complications: premature birth (35%), abortion or neonatal death (43%).
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · Q FEVER (part 2)

items.title: Management of Q Fever Fatigue Syndrome
items.content: Q fever fatigue syndrome affects up to 20% of patients post-acute Q fever, characterized by prolonged fatigue and symptoms like headaches and arthralgia. Long-term treatment with doxycycline does not reduce fatigue severity compared to placebo. Cognitive behavioral therapy has shown short-term effectiveness in reducing fatigue but lacks long-term benefits.
keyPoints: Q fever fatigue syndrome affects up to 20% of patients post-acute Q fever.
Symptoms include prolonged fatigue, headaches, sweats, arthralgia, myalgias.
Long-term doxycycline treatment does not reduce fatigue severity compared to placebo.
Cognitive behavioral therapy reduces fatigue short-term but not after 1 year.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · Q FEVER (part 2)

items.title: Recognizing Chronic Q Fever
items.content: Chronic Q fever develops in 1–5% of patients after primary infection, often unrecognized or asymptomatic. It manifests as endocarditis, aneurysms, or vascular prosthesis infections, with a median duration from acute to chronic infection of 13 months.
keyPoints: 1-5% of patients develop chronic Q fever post-primary infection
Median time from primary to chronic infection is 13 months
Risk factors include male sex, older age, valvulopathy, prior valve surgery, aneurysms, vascular prostheses, renal insufficiency, and immunocompromised state
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · Q FEVER (part 3)

items.title: Diagnosing Chronic Q Fever
items.content: Chronic Q fever diagnosis is challenging due to nonspecific symptoms. Diagnosis relies on serologic assays like IFA, PCR detection of C. burnetii DNA, and clinical criteria. High phase I IgG titer (>512) suggests chronic infection.
keyPoints: Diagnosis involves serologic assays (IFA as gold standard), PCR, and clinical criteria
High phase I IgG titer (>512) suggestive of chronic Q fever
Dutch consensus guideline recommended for diagnosis
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · Q FEVER (part 3)

items.title: Complications and Mortality in Chronic Q Fever
items.content: Chronic Q fever can lead to severe complications like acute aneurysms, heart failure, and noncardiac abscesses. Mortality rates are high: 25% overall, with the highest (33%) in patients with both endocarditis and vascular infection.
keyPoints: 65% of chronic Q fever cases develop complications
Common complications include acute aneurysms, heart failure, noncardiac abscesses
Mortality rates: 25% overall, 33% with both endocarditis and vascular infection

items.title: Role of FDG-PET/CT in Diagnosing Chronic Q Fever Endocarditis
items.content: FDG-PET/CT is valuable for detecting prosthetic and native valve infections, as well as other intravascular infections associated with chronic Q fever. Despite high specificity, its low sensitivity means a normal scan cannot exclude endocarditis.
keyPoints: Detection rates: 12% (transthoracic echocardiography) vs. 21–50% (transesophageal echocardiography).
FDG-PET/CT can detect prosthetic valvular infection, intravascular infections, osteomyelitis, and lymphadenitis.
High specificity but low sensitivity for native valve endocarditis.
20% of patients had a change in diagnosis or treatment after FDG-PET/CT.
Incorporating FDG uptake as a major criterion increased definite endocarditis diagnoses by 1.9-fold.
57% of follow-up scans led to treatment adjustments.

items.title: Antibiotic Susceptibility for C. burnetii in Q Fever Treatment
items.content: C. burnetii is susceptible to several antibiotics, including doxycycline, quinolones, TMP-SMX, and macrolides, which are effective treatment options.
keyPoints: In vitro susceptibility of C. burnetii includes: doxycycline, quinolones, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX), macrolides.

items.statement: Long-term treatment with doxycycline reduces fatigue severity in patients with Q fever fatigue syndrome.
items.explanation: A randomized controlled trial showed that long-term treatment with doxycycline did not reduce fatigue severity compared to placebo.

items.statement: Chronic Q fever endocarditis is characterized by endothelium-covered nodules on the valves.

items.statement: A normal FDG-PET/CT scan can exclude native valve endocarditis.
items.explanation: A normal FDG-PET/CT scan cannot exclude native valve endocarditis due to its low sensitivity.

items.statement: Adding FDG uptake in a heart valve as a major criterion to the Duke criteria increased definite endocarditis diagnoses by 1.9-fold.

items.assertion: Establishing the etiologic diagnosis of rickettsioses during the acute stage is straightforward.
items.reason: Definitive diagnosis usually requires examination of serum samples in both acute and convalescent phases.

items.assertion: Epidemiologic clues are important for diagnosing rickettsial diseases.
items.reason: These include environmental exposure to vectors and travel history to endemic regions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why epidemiologic clues are important.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · Rickettsial Diseases

items.assertion: Occlusive thrombosis and ischemic necrosis are the primary pathological bases for tissue injury in RMSF.
items.reason: Increased vascular permeability, with resulting edema, hypovolemia, and ischemia, is responsible for tissue and organ injury in RMSF.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; increased vascular permeability, not occlusive thrombosis and ischemic necrosis, causes tissue injury.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 1)

items.assertion: Rash in RMSF patients typically appears within the first three days of illness.
items.reason: Erythematous macules appear first on the wrists and ankles, then spread to other areas.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; rash is evident in only 14% of patients on the first day and 49% during the first three days.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 1)

items.assertion: Diagnosis of RMSF should be considered when a rash appears.
items.reason: RMSF can mimic viral infections early in the course, and worsening illness warrants reevaluation for RMSF.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why diagnosis should be considered with a rash.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 2)

items.assertion: Serologic tests for RMSF are usually positive at presentation.
items.reason: The sensitivity and specificity of the indirect immunofluorescence IgG assay are high.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; serologic tests are usually negative at presentation.
items.reference: Rickettsial Diseases · ROCKY MOUNTAIN SPOTTED FEVER (part 2)
